# Executive Summary

```{r index-setup, include=FALSE}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# R8: Nonfatal by industry
r8_data <- read_excel(
  "case-and-demographic-characteristics-table-r8-2023-2024.xlsx",
  col_names = FALSE)[-c(1,2), ]

names(r8_data)[1]  <- "industry"
names(r8_data)[2]  <- "naics"
names(r8_data)[3]  <- "case_type"
names(r8_data)[4]  <- "total_rate"
names(r8_data)[10] <- "fall_rate"

mfg <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "31-33", case_type == "DAFW")
fin <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "52",    case_type == "DAFW")
pro <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "54",    case_type == "DAFW")

mfg_total <- as.numeric(mfg$total_rate[1])
mfg_falls <- as.numeric(mfg$fall_rate[1])
fin_total  <- as.numeric(fin$total_rate[1])
fin_falls  <- as.numeric(fin$fall_rate[1])
pro_total  <- as.numeric(pro$total_rate[1])
pro_falls  <- as.numeric(pro$fall_rate[1])

mfg_fall_share <- round(mfg_falls / mfg_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_share  <- round(fin_falls  / fin_total  * 100, 1)
pro_fall_share  <- round(pro_falls  / pro_total  * 100, 1)

# Fatal: extracted from file 
fatal_df <- as.data.frame(
  read_excel("fatal.xlsx", col_names = FALSE))

mfg_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[143, 3])
mfg_fatal_falls <- as.numeric(fatal_df[143, 7])
fin_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[453, 3])
fin_fatal_falls <- as.numeric(fatal_df[453, 7])
pro_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[488, 3])
pro_fatal_falls <- as.numeric(fatal_df[488, 7])

mfg_fall_pct <- round(mfg_fatal_falls / mfg_fatal_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_pct  <- round(fin_fatal_falls  / fin_fatal_total  * 100, 1)
pro_fall_pct  <- round(pro_fatal_falls  / pro_fatal_total  * 100, 1)
```

This report compares workplace injury risk between manufacturing workers and
office-sector workers using 2023–2024 federal data from the U.S. Bureau of
Labor Statistics. Two datasets are used: the Survey of Occupational Injuries
and Illnesses (SOII, Table R8) for nonfatal injuries, and the Census of Fatal
Occupational Injuries (CFOI) for workplace deaths.

::: {.callout-note}
## Research Question
Are office workers at greater risk of injury — particularly falls — than
manufacturing workers, and how does injury severity compare?
:::

## Key Findings

- **Manufacturing workers are injured far more often overall.** The nonfatal
  injury rate in manufacturing is `r mfg_total` per 10,000 workers —
  `r round(mfg_total / fin_total, 0)` times higher than in finance and
  insurance (`r fin_total`).

- **Falls dominate in office environments.** Falls account for
  `r fin_fall_share`% of all nonfatal injuries in finance and insurance,
  compared to just `r mfg_fall_share`% in manufacturing. When an office
  worker is injured, it is most likely from a fall.

- **The fatal data confirms the pattern.** Falls caused `r fin_fall_pct`% of
  deaths in finance and insurance versus `r mfg_fall_pct`% in manufacturing —
  consistent across both nonfatal and fatal measures.

::: {.callout-important}
## Recommendation
Safety officers in office environments should treat fall prevention as a top
priority even though overall injury counts appear low. Targeted measures such as 
slip-resistant flooring, clear walkway policies, and employee awareness programs can meaningfully reduce this risk.
:::

<!-- Added bottom ::: to close the callout -->
